data.title: Archer Aviation: Leaving No Room For Disappointment
data.description:
Archer Aviation: Needs To Convert Growth Prospects Into Revenue

It seems like speculative investors just refuse to give up, riding on the tech-driven advance that has driven the S&P 500 (SPX, SPY) to new highs in August. The stars have also started to align for the leading eVTOL companies like Archer Aviation Inc. (NYSE:ACHR), paving the way toward commercialization in the U.S. and in international markets.

With a growing order book spanning beyond the domestic market opportunity, Archer Aviation appears to be priming for an accelerated scale up and deployment phase. The beaming prospects could see the company capture substantive benefits from a burgeoning customer base keen on leveraging the nascent, but high-growth industry, offering ACHR lucrative first mover advantages into what is a tightly regulated opportunity with multiple layers of certification, helping to establish a robust and defensible moat.

In my last Archer Aviation writeup, I articulated the need to remain circumspect even though I appreciated the upcoming commercial launches, while expanding its reach into the defense industrial base with capable and well-connected partners.

Archer Aviation: Timeline For Full Certification Will Be Scrutinized

Well, the good news is that ACHR's bullish narrative seems to have stayed on the right track. I believe the certification process remains the main bugbear in hampering even more aggressive buying sentiments from bullish investors. Accordingly, management articulated that while they are still working closely with the FAA in "conducting reviews," the compliance completion process has reached the 15% approval mark. Hence, execution risks on the exact timing for the full approval cannot be fully ascertained, although management doesn't appear to be concerned about not being able to obtain the necessary certification.

Archer Aviation has highlighted that it's also ramping up in preparation for the LA28 Olympics, compressing the timeline for achieving full FAA certification to about the 1,000-day mark. Hence, the battle with arch-rival Joby Aviation (JOBY) could define the opening eVTOL economics in the hotly contested domestic market, given the multibillion order book that Archer Aviation has already secured in the U.S.

Hence, I believe investors must pay close attention to crucial phases in its U.S. launch program, with critical junctures including TIA flight testing as one of the pivotal milestones that the company is aiming to secure in the near term. In addition, the ability to attain the production certificate will also be another crucial checkpoint in its certification ramp up program, based on the current FAA inspection underway for its Silicon Valley and Georgia manufacturing facilities.

Hence, I think it's opportune to know that ACHR's commercial opportunity is predicated on its ability to capture the emerging eVTOL economics beyond the shores of the U.S. And the primary UAE launch market appears to be pacing well for a further scale-up, having already delivered its first Midnight aircraft while also conducted tests in the harsh July weather.

Notably, the company also emphasized that the current initiative with Abu Dhabi Aviation is expected to gain further traction in the next 18 to 24 months, with scheduled milestone payments worth "tens of millions of dollars" starting from the second half of 2025. So, I think it does show that a solidly successful UAE launch effort could help mitigate investor worries about delayed FAA final phase certification efforts that could stymie buying sentiments at a crucial juncture.

In addition, the fledgling international launch program predicated on the partnerships with Ethiopia, and Indonesia should also help provide early revenue accretion efforts, while helping to validate its capabilities to other launch markets in the future.

Buy Or Sell ACHR Stock?

As a result, I believe the optimistic modeling by Wall Street analysts on a rapid acceleration in revenue through the 2029 forecast period seems reasonable, given the multi-year scaling framework promulgated by management. However, it's also critical to note that while the $1.7B in liquidity might be sufficient to tank the $1.44B in cash burn through FY2028, it also depends very much on the scale up economics from 2026, pending FAA certification.

Consider that the company's growing order book also consists of a $1.5B deal with United Airlines, with a program spanning across multiple key hubs. However, it's crucial to note that backlog doesn't automatically convert into revenue, suggesting that it still depends on overcoming the various obstacles (and not just certification) that could still hamper a more confident ramp up in the next few years.

With an FY2029 revenue multiple of 3.6x already markedly more than the sector's median on a forward basis of 1.67x, I believe the market has already baked in substantial optimism on a multi-year rollout. Hence, unless management provides much higher growth prospects with a more significant backlog through the FY2029 forecast period, it might not be enough to spur much more aggressive buying, if the execution falters, or unanticipated delays encumbered Archer Aviation's multiple launch programs.

With that in mind, I still view the risk/reward as unfavorable for investors thinking about entering Archer Aviation Inc. stock now. I exhort them to demonstrate more patience for the company to work through the certification progress, while also biding their time for a more pronounced pullback before deciding whether to pull the buy trigger.

Rating: Maintain Hold.

data.title: Why WPP PLC Is Likely The Best Opportunity In Advertising
data.description:
In this article, I'll be revisiting one of my previous, fairly recent pieces from about 2.5 months back, and show you why I consider WPP PLC (NYSE:WPP) to still be a "BUY" worthy of consideration despite a non-trivial decline and underperformance relative to the SPY in a short time. My position is, because of the returns seen here, in the red. Does that mean I'm not buying more?

Obviously, I wish I could have bought the company at a better valuation - but I take solace in the fact that, because of what I said in my last piece, where I viewed both Omnicom (OMC) and Interpublic (IPG) as better stability-oriented alternatives, my position in WPP is rather small.

But that is about to change.

I believe the recent move from the company has resulted in WPP now being significantly undervalued, with an upside of well over 25% conservatively on an annual basis. And that, dear readers, is something that catches my attention. Not just because of stellar fundamentals, including a BBB rating and a now-8-9% yield (even with a cut, that yield is solid), but because of the valuation it's trading at now, which is so dirt cheap that it's now trading close to book value.

Let's look at the company's upside and valuation, as well as recent news here (what sort of reasons we have for that decline).

WPP PLC - The now-best upside in advertising.

WPP isn't my biggest position - at least not yet. And the reason that the company saw a relatively significant decline can be found in the company's recent results. Not only margins and profit, but also top-line results and revenues saw declines as well.

Why?

No strange reasons, really. In fact, the reasons that I have covered in the past, and not only for WPP PLC, but for many advertising agencies. We're talking about macroeconomic uncertainty that is driving a certain carefulness in client spending, but also impacting a slower rate of growth for new business. This, combined with the business model that these companies work under, meaning they have relatively short contract cycles, means that it does not take that long for things to go "down". The company, however, won't be fundamentally derailed by a few bad quarters - or even by a few bad years.

The market is currently focusing on the perception of increased risk from a higher volatility coupled with a higher leverage than its peers, as well as a higher dividend yield than its peers.

However, WPP has already taken steps to reduce this - cutting staff costs, reducing back-office costs, and working to invest in WPP Open, AI, and data analytics.

This is not to say that a near 300 bps (3%) decline in operating margin is not serious. Such a decline is in fact very serious, especially in the short term. It's not just declining revenue, but an increase in severance costs as the company is trying to quickly right-size its staffing.

The strategic actions taken include rebranding for WPP media, simplification, and Open Intelligence. The company's work has given results, and almost 70,000 users are now using WPP open, and new partnerships have been made, including ones with TikTok, as well as with products.

In terms of geography, almost every region is down, with the worst declines in the Asian region, but most of the segments are down over 5%. In terms of end markets, only CPG and automotive are seeing real growth, together with Telco. The declines in most of the markets, especially things like "other" with declines of over 15% in a YoY period, are quite severe and definitely worth noting. When focusing on these singular trends, understanding the declines the company is going through becomes somewhat easier.

However, when looking at precisely what is driving these declines, we quickly see that most of the bottom line reduction is actually Staff costs and severance costs. Barring that, while not positive, the negative operating margin would have been a positive one, up about 0.5% for the period by the company's current estimates.

Restructuring costs are ongoing, and we shouldn't expect them to decline significantly going forward either. For 2024-2025 the company has incurred over £100M of them, both related to property, IT, and personnel. One positive thing that can be noted is that at least the company's leverage isn't increased - as of the interim period, the company's adjusted net debt is actually down close to £150M, thanks to Net M&A and disposals as well as fairly solid working capital levels.

The company's updated forecast is no doubt part of the reason why things are relatively negative on the market as well. The company now expects a decline of up to 5%, with a further decline or operating margin of up to 175 bps for the full-year period, excluding FX, which presents another risk.

The market is interpreting this as fundamentally problematic enough to justify this BBB-rated company trading at something less than 5x P/E. Whenever a quality company, by which I mean credit rating as well as other factors, starts trading below 5-6x P/E, I tend to pay attention. That's the sort of valuation that allowed me to significantly outperform with insurance businesses like Unum (UNM) and Lincoln National (LNC), both generating triple-digit rates of return provided you bought them at that valuation.

While WPP is facing some challenges, it's nowhere near as serious as expected here. The current analyst forecasts call for a decline of around 20% this year, and with the 30%+ decline in share price, I believe that is already priced in at this time. The forecast is also for a slight right-sizing of the company dividend, which I would consider to be a good idea given the lower earnings. It should be mentioned, though, that even the current company dividend is covered in an ample manner (less than 80% payout) by the forecasted 2025E earnings level, as well as the next few years of earnings.

Looking at statistics, WPP PLC is one of the hardest Advertising agencies to accurately forecast - the company does negatively miss from time to time, and this needs to be included in the thesis as well (but is actually included in my share price and estimate).

As always, the market tends to throw the baby out with the bathwater. It is ignoring many of the advantages that the company now, indisputably, has.

The company is dropping in revenue and profit losses due to loss of clients, loss of revenues, and similar trends. I argue that the reaction marks a lack of understanding for the business model in advertising, which is both shorter-term and cyclical, and thus no stranger to ups and downs. While perhaps a bit more clients went away here than usual, this has not put WPP PLC in a situation where this sort of valuation can be said to be justified.

And that is why I am going in with a "BUY" here, bordering on a "STRONG BUY" rating.

Let me show you what I mean.

WPP PLC - Now the best opportunity, even with the volatility taken into consideration

Previously, I argued that OMC and IPG were better plays than WPP, while saying that Publicis (OTCQX:PUBGY) is not investable. The latter of that thesis still holds - the former is now changed.

The appeal of WPP can be seen at any valuation at this price, even if you estimate a 1-2% EPS decline on an annualized basis to 2027E. And while it's not a desirable forecast, to invest in a company that is being viewed as declining, it's all a matter of what you pay.

As of this time, we're at below £3.7/share for the native ticker on the LSE, WPP. This makes a yield of almost 8.7%, and an EPS yield of over 20%, the inverse of P/E. The only reason to ignore this company at this juncture would be a serious concern about its survival, which I do not have (but if you do, I'd love to hear it).

Even at a sub-6x forward P/E and assuming an EPS decline, this company's returns are now positive, if not market-beating.

And you'd have to be very conservative to put the company at something like a £4/share fair value. I would put it closer to double that - around £8/share, even with the lower estimates, which puts my updated target at £5.8/share, down from £6.5/share from my last article.

My fair value of £8/share represents roughly 10.5x P/E, which is still below the 5-year average of close to 11x, and significantly below the 20-year average of 13x P/E. At 13x P/E, this company's upside is closer to 180% or 54% per year.

While the advertising sector is undervalued here, the undervaluation we're seeing here is downright ridiculous to me. This is reflected in analyst targets. For the native LSE ticker, analysts are currently putting the company at a range of £3.5/share to £5.8, close to my own PT. However, that is down from a high PT of almost £12/share less than a year ago. So the question becomes, has the company done something to become worth less than half in less than a year?

I would argue that it has not, and that this is a severe overreaction for one of the largest advertising businesses on the planet. On multiple basis, you're paying less than 0.66x to sales, almost 1x revenue, and less than 2.5x value to book. At any time in its history, if you invested at such a multiple, you'd have seen a market-beating rate of return.

And I don't think it will be different now.

While my position currently is 30% in the negative is not something I enjoy seeing, but this is part of being a valuation-oriented investor. As I believe the company will recover, it does not faze me in the least.

We may be in for several years' worth of less-than-ideal results, but I believe the fundamentals of the company, as well as the "core" of advertising as a whole, will remain as it has in the last century or so - in the hands of the "larger" companies like WPP.

Risks to this thesis are as follows.

Risks & Considerations

Bearish arguments would be considered a change in advertising as a whole. If you believe that advertising is going through fundamental changes and is likely to recover, then that may be an argument for you not to invest here. Furthermore, there's the potential for the company to perform less than expected. You need to be "okay" with sub-par returns, beyond the company's impressive yield.

As long as you are, I believe one can invest with conviction and confidence into WPP:

Thesis

  • WPP PLC is a market-leading advertising company with sound fundamentals, including but not limited to a BBB rating and a very high, solid yield of over 8.5% that I consider well-covered even with what the company is going through at the moment.
  • Using current estimates, I give the company a native PT of £5.8/share, down from £6.5/share due to poor results. This comes to a "BUY" rating for the company at this particular time, translating to a NYSE target of $39/share.
  • What has changed is that the company, even aware of its risks, is now undoubtedly cheap with an earnings yield of over 20%, and is one of the most undervalued states I've seen a large advertising company in for the past 10 years. That makes the company a high-conviction "BUY" for me.

Here are my criteria and how the company fulfills them (italicized).

  • This company is overall qualitative.
  • This company is fundamentally safe/conservative & well-run.
  • This company pays a well-covered dividend.
  • This company is currently cheap.
  • This company has a realistic upside that is high enough, based on earnings growth or multiple expansions/reversions.

The company fulfills 3 out of 3 quality indicators and 1 out of 2 valuation indicators. That means 4/5 "stars" here, which is enough for an attractive thesis for the company. Based on this, and a valuation target of £5.8/share where I see a risk/reward-adjusted upside that's attractive enough for me. I give the company a “Buy” rating as of August 2025.

data.title: OMAH: I'd Rather Just Own Berkshire Hathaway
data.description:
VistaShares Target 15 Berkshire Select Income ETF (NYSEARCA:OMAH) is a unique covered call ETF, based not on a stock market index, but rather Berkshire Hathaway’s (BRK.B) underlying stock portfolio. The way the fund works is it invests in a diversified portfolio consisting of Berkshire’s top 20 portfolio shares. The fund invests in these shares, and writes covered calls based on them, aiming for a 15% yield–which it looks likely to hit based on the payouts made so far this year (the fund launched in 2025, so I can’t comment on its historical annual yields).

The first flaw in the OMAH ETF can be found in its name:

“Berkshire Select.”

Having Berkshire in the fund name would lead you to think that the fund is primarily built on Berkshire Hathaway, or at least a proxy for Berkshire Hathaway. A person could easily be led to think that, because every stock in the fund’s portfolio is a Berkshire holding. However, with 55% of Berkshire’s securities portfolio in cash/treasuries, and a large chunk of its assets in wholly owned companies, it's questionable whether a “copy Buffett stocks” portfolio is a Berkshire Hathaway proxy. The assets that OMAH holds represent only a moderate-sized percentage of Berkshire’s balance sheet. Also, OMAH only holds about a third of Berkshire’s stocks (a larger percentage going by market cap weighting). It does hold a 10% weighted position in Berkshire itself, but that’s the only exposure it has to Berkshire’s cash position and wholly owned businesses.

A second flaw with the concept of a Berkshire-based covered call fund, is the nature of Berkshire Hathaway as a corporation rather than an index ETF. Because Berkshire is a corporation, its managers are free to buy and sell stocks whenever they see fit, and even completely exit equities if they wish to. The company only has to disclose its trades once every three months, and even occasionally gets permission to not disclose trades until done building its position! An index fund, by contrast, tracks an index whose components are chosen in public in a fairly transparent way. So, OMAH’s fund managers are not nearly as able to track their “benchmark” as a typical covered call index ETF’s managers are.

Despite these issues, ETFs like OMAH do have a few things going for them. They offer more yield than their underlying benchmarks; they provide some hedging benefits; and they reduce portfolio volatility. These qualities are certainly not nothing, but they on their own aren’t enough to justify an investment. An investor needs a solid grasp on the underlying stocks or index before he can make an informed investment in a covered call ETF.

As for OMAH specifically: it most likely delivers all of the benefits that covered call ETFs do. However, as an extremely young fund, it is unproven at this point. Additionally, if investors seek exposure to Berkshire Hathaway for the sake of maximizing returns–which is a pretty common goal for holders of individual stocks–then the covered call yield enhancement works against their investment objective: covered call writing reduces volatility, but it caps returns in normal market conditions.

For the most part, I’ve given bullish ratings to covered call ETFs this year, the reason being that the indexes underlying them are generally quite pricey, so the yield enhancement provides some desirable hedging benefits. I stand by those calls. However, Berkshire Hathaway itself is not so pricey, isn't particularly volatile, and usually delivers considerable capital gains. For this reason I’m initiating coverage of OMAH at hold.

Portfolio Composition

As mentioned previously, OMAH’s portfolio is designed to replicate the public equity portion of Berkshire Hathaway’s portfolio. As such, the portfolio has a value tilt and leans toward large cap wide moat names. Among its largest holdings are these:

  • Apple (AAPL).

  • Berkshire Hathaway.

  • Chevron (CVX).

  • American Express (AXP).

  • Coca-Cola (KO).

  • Moody’s (MCO).

So, basically, a lot of financials, energy and Apple. Now, let’s look at some valuation and competitive metrics for these stocks. As a proxy for “competitive strength,” I will use Morningstar (MORN) moat ratings; for value, I will use metrics from Seeking Alpha Quant.

As you can see, the portfolio leans toward wide moat names, with a slightly lower average P/E ratio than the S&P 500. On the other hand, these stocks are not all that value tilted by some other metrics, such as the average price to book ratio, which is actually slightly higher than that of the S&P 500 right now. Nevertheless, these are mostly quality companies trading at fairly sensible valuations.

The long portfolio is probably the best thing that OMAH has going for it. It’s reasonably diversified and leans toward sensibly valued, high quality names. With that established, we can now look at the options portion of OMAH’s portfolio.

Covered Call Yield Enhancement

OMAH uses a covered call yield enhancement strategy. As I wrote in previous articles about covered call funds, such options give you an option premium in exchange for agreeing to sell your shares at a set price. At times, the income gained from writing them can be high.

The specifics of OMAH’s covered call strategy are as follows:

  • The fund managers write out of the money calls.

  • Because the calls are out of the money, there is a chance that they will expire worthless, in which case investors keep the premium as an extra “dividend.”

  • If stock prices rise, then the fund’s managers are forced to sell stock, but still keep the premium.

  • The fund’s managers aim for a 15% return using covered calls and underlying portfolio dividends.

Is the strategy working?

While OMAH is too young to say for sure, the last five months’ distributions would indeed produce a 15% yield at today’s price, if continued for another seven months.

So, OMAH looks set to hit its income goal.

The problem is the rationale of seeking high income from a Berkshire-mimicking portfolio. Berkshire is famed for its excellent capital allocation, and ability to deliver positive gains even in bear markets. The same goes for stocks in Berkshire’s portfolio. The company already has ample risk management built in, in the form of a high cash allocation and meticulous security selection. If the company continues with these strategies, then Berkshire covered calls will be exercised frequently, as Berkshire will likely be making significant capital gains. So, a Berkshire-like portfolio is probably not the best one for the covered call strategy.

Basic Characteristics

Before concluding, a word on OMAH’s basic technical characteristics is in order. These characteristics–such as the management expense ratio (MER) and bid-ask spread–influence investors’ returns. They include:

  • A 0.95% MER.

  • 21 stocks.

  • A 15% distribution rate.

  • No discount or premium to net asset value (NAV).

  • A 0.05% bid-ask spread.

Broadly, these characteristics suggest that OMAH is a pretty pricey ETF. The fee is nearly a full percent and the bid-ask spread is above average. The amount of diversification is less than what you’d get in a broad market fund, and the distribution rate, while high, comes from capping upside.

The Bottom Line

The bottom line on the VistaShares Target 15 Berkshire Select Income ETF is that it’s just not the best covered call fund out there. The fees are quite high and the underlying holdings are high quality and likely to deliver capital gains. The fund’s yield enhancement strategy cuts off much of those gains. Additionally, it's not clear that OMAH will be able to perfectly replicate Berkshire's holdings, as some of them are wholly owned Berkshire subsidiaries, while the publicly traded ones are reported just once per quarter. Overall, I’d rather just own Buffett stocks unhedged than hold OMAH.

data.title: SDIV: High Yield Stocks And High Risk Pair Up In This ETF
data.description:
Investing for yield is one of the most popular motivations for investors to do research, and make their ultimate investing decisions. Yield comes in many flavors, from ultra-safe cash-like investments, to high yielding lower-quality bonds. Within the global stock market, there are a range of choices. Some stocks do not pay any dividends, quarterly, semi-annually, or otherwise. Typically that is either because management is more focused on growing earnings, or because the company does not have sufficient cash flow and reserves to afford regular payouts to shareholders.

Dividend-paying stocks have been a popular investing choice, and that is likely to continue. Because a dividend payment, while it reduces the stock price by the amount of that dividend per share, is a way for investors to get paid regularly. Essentially, they are sharing in the profits of the business, without having to make a transaction to get the cash. While the dividend-paying ability of a company is part of how the market values a stock, in the 21st century, companies have been more willing to tap the capital markets to borrow. While some of that borrowing aims to make purchases of compatible businesses, or to fund new growth projects, some of it ends up shoring up available liquidity to keep the dividend payments high enough to keep investor confidence elevated.

While this is all transparent in a public company's financial statement, what has changed since the 20th century, and as this current century has continued, is the frequency with which dividend payments are delivered with some risk they will not continue. But dividend investing grew so popular, the idea of "high yield stocks" has taken their spot alongside high yield bonds, as a sweet spot for many investors. In particular, those in retirement or getting close to it.

The Global X SuperDividend ETF (NYSEARCA:SDIV) is structured to be one of the highest-yielding dividend ETFs available. It is based on the Solactive SuperDividend Index, which is described just below. However, before reviewing that methodology and SDIV in more detail, take a glance at this comparison of the ETF's top 10 holdings, 10 years apart (2025 and 2015).

There's not a single common holding. This reminds us that very high-yielding equities are different. And so is investing in them. Most companies cannot sustain a very high dividend payment, since they often result from either temporary concerns about the business driving the stock price down and yield up. Or, if the stock becomes too popular, moving its price higher, the yield drops. That is, unless it is a rare case of a stock with a high yield growing its profits and dividend payment at a high rate. That is not impossible, but it is quite rare.

SDIV: high yield often means lower quality

The bottom line is that SDIV represents a set of stocks that are likely of less than stellar financial condition. "Junk bonds" are well-known. And while SDIV is not intended to be the home of "junk stocks," for a stock to be in the portfolio, there's a good chance it is of moderate to lower quality.

However, this is not the only reason a stock makes SDIV's filter. It is typically an ETF with a high non-US stock weighting, since US stocks in aggregate tend to not be the highest yielders. This chart shows the history of US large cap, non-US developed market stocks and emerging market stocks from the start of 2009 (the tail end of the global financial crisis) through mid-2025. This is one time period, but it indicates what has been a consistent relationship between these equity asset classes in terms of dividend yield over time. We see that US stocks tend to bring up the rear here.

During a similar era, one which accommodates SDIV's 2011 inception date, we see just how high SDIV's yield tends to be versus the broader US market, as well as its sister ETF from Global X (DIV), which is limited to considering US stocks and has a low-volatility screen included. SDIV should also be expected to out-yield high yield bond index ETFs.

But does that make it an investment anyone should consider? All investing is a personalized choice, but SDIV has some clear structural advantages and tradeoffs. Those should be understood by anyone seeking to try to get a piece of that high stock yield exposure.

As shown here, this 100-stock portfolio pays dividends monthly, something that at one time made it a pioneer in the space. Over time, ETF firms responded to the deluge of retirement savers, to the point where monthly-paying dividend ETFs are not difficult to find. US stocks typically pay dividends quarterly, and non-US stocks often pay semi-annually. So the portfolio must be calibrated by Global X to manage that monthly payout.

The following two graphics are as of a point in time, but intended to provide a sense of SDIV's diversification features. First, the sector exposure indicates that high yield stocks can be found just about anywhere. But certain sectors like energy, REITs and financial stocks tend to occupy a decent portion of the ETF's assets.

SDIV is global, and there have been times when the US was a large part of the total allocation, or smaller than what is shown below. The key to this visual is that there are many countries whose stocks are considered for SDIV. So much so that a list of top countries does not contain them all.

How are stocks chosen for the Global X SuperDividend ETF?

SDIV shops for two different types of stocks. If they are part of the benchmark index noted above, a yield of at least 3% is required. That hurdle increases for stocks outside that benchmark, to a range from 6% to 20%. Ultimately, SDIV's passive managers are looking for high yield stocks where they can get them, even if some of their portfolio reaches outside its main screening list. This underscores the fact that this ETF is not focused on fundamental quality. It aims for higher yield. That said, most stocks yielding more then 20% are too speculative even for SDIV's portfolio.

Companies must also have a market capitalization of $500 million or more in US Dollars. That is much more lenient than most dividend ETFs, as it allows for "microcap" stocks. The same can be said for the low bar set for Average Daily Volume in the last three months. A stock need only trade $1 million to be considered.

SDIV insists on "stable" dividend payments, but only to the extent that the company has not made an official announcement about canceling or reducing its dividend per share. That opens the ETF up to both potential event risk, but also to capturing very high yields from stocks that turn out to be OK fundamentally.

SDIV does exclude closed-end funds, partnerships and Business Development Companies (BDCs), which makes it somewhat compatible alongside ETFs that focus on such mandates.

As SDIV states, here's what happens from there:

The companies in the Selection Pool are ranked in descending order according to their Dividend Yield. The 100 companies with the largest Dividend Yield on that day are then chosen as Index Components. • On the annual Adjustment Days, if a company is included in the Selection Pool, it is only removed from the index composition if its Dividend Yield on the Selection Day is not in the Top 200 of the Selection Pool. In this case, the company with the largest Dividend Yield that is currently not an index member is chosen as replacement. • Once a quarter (ten business days before the last trading day in May, August and November), the index components are screened for dividend cuts or an overall negative outlook concerning the companies’ dividend policy. Companies may be excluded on these quarterly reviews and will be replaced with the top ranked company from the selection pool that is currently not an index member.

SDIV: for the bold equity income seeker to consider

SDIV has shown the ability to move by 40% in either direction with 12 months' time. Similar to the S&P 500, but not always in sync with it.

Investors can consider this ETF, but anyone doing so should have plenty of caution flags. Those willing to accept the risks will get a very high current return via the monthly dividend payments from SDIV. That comes with plenty of volatility. As usual with investing, it is all about the tradeoffs.

This article answers these three main questions about SDIV:

  • Does SDIV carry greater risk than the average dividend focused ETF?
  • How does SDIV's yield compare to other ETFs?
  • Should investors considering SDIV expect greater volatility?

Editor's note: This article is intended to provide a general overview of the ETF for educational purposes only and, unlike other articles on Seeking Alpha, does not offer an investment opinion about the ETF.
