data.title: ZJK Industrial: Speculative AI Angle And Expensive Valuation Make Me Bearish
data.description:
ZJK Industrial Co., Ltd. (NASDAQ:ZJK) is a producer of hardware components for high-technology industries and applications. It was granted a Chinese patent for an image recognition-based inspection system that improves automation and efficiency in its manufacturing processes. ZJK also seems to be shifting its technology towards being closely aligned with AI chip production processes. They also showcased a prototype for liquid cooling components that could be helpful for AI chips in general, showing they could eventually play a role in higher-value segments of AI infrastructure. Still, for the most part, those larger aims appear speculative at this stage. And in the meantime, ZJK’s stock trades at expensive multiples with a relatively short cash runway. Hence, I lean bearish on ZJK for now.

High-Tech Inputs

ZJK Industrial is essentially a manufacturer of hardware products for industries that require highly advanced technology. It originated from Shenzhen Zhongjinke Hardware Products Co., Ltd. (2011 - 2024), and it changed its name before its NASDAQ listing in September 2024. ZJK is currently headquartered in Shenzhen, China, which makes it a foreign issuer, and is why the 2024 full-year financials are its latest updated figures for the time being.

Moreover, ZJK’s product categories include screws, turned parts, and stamping parts. In this case, ZJK’s Screws are precision fasteners specialized for electronics, automotive, and aerospace applications. They are manufactured via cold heading, followed by thread rolling, and sometimes surface treatments like PVD coating or anti-corrosion plating. These highly specialized screws are vital for certain consumer electronics, automotive EVs, AI infrastructure, and aerospace solutions. In fact, this is part of ZJK’s main business as it sells them in large volumes. Although such screws often convey lower margins and typically involve discounts for its clients, which is why it’s a high-volume, low-margin item.

As for its turned parts, these are ZJK’s precision metal components that start as round rods or bars and are turned to exact dimensions (hence “turned” into something specialized). Their turned parts are often used in AI data centers and servers, as structural mounts and connectors. Similarly, automotive EVs frequently require these customized parts for their sensors. In fact, turned parts are even useful in housing and brake components, as well as aerospace industries with precision alignment pins and coupling parts. Even medical devices employ these items as custom metal components.

With that in mind, ZJK’s turned parts are particularly promising since they have higher margins than screws. This is mostly because they’re customized and have much greater complexity than screws. Moreover, since 2024, ZJK has experienced increased demand for personalized items due to AI-related projects requiring parts with higher specs. In my view, this is a trend that will likely persist, which bodes promising for them in the long run since that allows ZJK to charge premium prices.

Lastly, ZJK’s stamping parts are flat metal sheets shaped into parts using stamping presses and dies. Like their turned parts, ZJK’s stamped items segment experienced a substantial increase in sales volume (+50.62%) and price (+99.07%) due to new AI-related contracts in 2024. However, these items are mostly used as inputs for brackets, frames, shields, connectors, and structural supports. Still, they’ve quickly become vital for AI infrastructure, automotive EVs, aerospace, and consumer electronics.

Automation Patent

Having said that, in May 2025, ZJK announced it received a Chinese patent for a new inspection-control system that uses recognition technology. This inspection system can check surface-mount technology (SMT) carrier tape packaging. This method essentially speeds up the production of electronic circuits, as it helps with in-line tape inspections in components directly placed on circuit boards. ZJK’s tech introduces an “auto-halt” feature whenever defects are detected. As you might imagine, this approach can significantly boost manufacturing automation capabilities through improved speed, accuracy, and quality control.

So basically, ZJK’s new patent is key to its SMT-related portfolio in AI hardware (and other high-tech electronics), as it helps with the inspecting process in production. I think it can potentially contribute help with cost savings as inspection labor costs can be substantially reduced, while also cutting rework expenses on faulty items. It’s also worth mentioning that ZJK previously showcased in a conference some samples for a liquid-cooling manifold project. Even if this wasn’t a formal collaboration (and only involved samples), it still aligned ZJK with a critical bottleneck in AI infrastructure (i.e., cooling). And the interesting aspect about these samples is that they’re less subject to US export control than AI chips themselves.

You see, advanced computing items and chips in general are often met with strict export controls, especially if their end-use is for AI chips in China. So, ZJK’s liquid-cooling items are closer to being classified as a mechanical component, which often doesn’t require the same licenses and permissions. Naturally, if the end-use is for AI chips, then export restrictions will still apply. So, this makes it unlikely that ZJK will be able to simply bypass export restrictions using “samples” as a loophole in a large-scale chip production process. Nevertheless, it does seem ZJK will diversify beyond commodity parts into higher-value AI manufacturing.

It is worth noting that AI chips still require advanced cooling solutions, which is why ZJK can still play a key role. But more importantly, ZJK acquired and developed a highly specialized team when developing its liquid cooling parts samples. However, as far as we know, this initiative remains in the prototype and testing phase.

Valuation And Risk Analysis

And, as you might imagine, if ZJK actually secured a larger deal with a major chip maker for its inspecting patents, it could be a game-changer for the stock. After all, being responsible for a vital portion of the AI supply chain could be incredibly valuable. For example, ZJK stock soared shortly after it seemed like they were close to sealing this type of deal.

Unfortunately, based on the publicly available information today, a large-scale collaboration like this still seems highly unlikely. In any event, ZJK currently trades at a $201.3 million market cap. In fact, the shares have declined by around 82% from their ATH. And the latest press release from April 2025 shows their balance sheet holds only $12.3 million in cash and equivalents, plus $2.6 million in short-term investments. That amounts to roughly $14.9 million in available liquidity for their R&D and operations, against around $3.1 million in financial debt (aside from other regular operating liabilities).

Also, their book value reached $30.1 million, indicating they still have a high P/B of 6.7. For comparison, ZJK’s sector’s median P/B is closer to 3.0. Likewise, their 2024 full-year revenues came in at $37.8 million, implying an expensive P/S of 5.3. This is again notably higher than its peers’ P/S of 1.7. So, surprisingly enough, even after the massive decline in its stock price, ZJK still seems somewhat overvalued.

I also estimate ZJK’s Q4 2024 alone burned through $2.3 million in cash. Note that I got that figure by simply adding its quarterly cash flows from operations and CAPEX. Yet, that suggests an annual burn rate of approximately $9.2 million, which implies a short cash runway of just 1.6 years. So, on balance, ZJK still seems expensive, and its runway could become an issue in the near term. Hence, I have to lean towards a “Sell” rating based on these risks despite their promising patent.

Conclusion: Not Worth The Risk

In my view, it’s probably unwise to short the stock after a massive 82% decline, especially since it’s a thinly traded foreign issuer that can quickly bounce on positive headlines rather than hint at more partnerships. Seeking Alpha’s dashboard on ZJK shows its trading volume is around 12.5 thousand shares, which at the current PPS of $3.20 translates to just $40.0 thousand in trading liquidity. That means even tiny buy/sell orders can significantly push the price. I also imagine their patent can be highly valuable in chip inspecting processes, though it should be incorporated into a large-scale production chain to fully realize its potential. So, for now, I think it’s best to simply stay away from ZJK and keep it on a watchlist.

data.title: Disney's Strategy For Japan's Anime Boom Is Welcome
data.description:
Just recently, I wrote an article regarding the deal struck between Webtoon Entertainment (WBTN) and The Walt Disney Company (NYSE:DIS) whereby the latter will provide existing and new content to the former in what the management team at Webtoon Entertainment called a strategic and financial win for both parties. The primary focus in that article was specifically on Webtoon Entertainment. But it did serve as the foundation for the idea to write an article dedicated specifically to the opportunity that exists for Disney when it comes to the anime space.

Many western entertainment businesses have been moving more heavily into this industry even as the Japanese government is pushing toward its growth. In some respects, this is a rare time when Disney has fallen behind the competition. But given the company's significant financial resources, it certainly has the chance to grow in this market moving forward. Add on top of this how attractive Disney is, especially in light of recent financial performance, and I believe that the company continues to deserve the "Strong buy" rating I have had it at for quite some time now.

Disney's big opportunity

Instead of rehashing all the details regarding the relationship that now exists between Disney and Webtoon Entertainment, I would just refer you to the aforementioned article that I wrote about that deal. More broadly, I think that a discussion of the anime industry and what it could mean for a firm such as Disney is warranted. For starters, while this might seem like a niche segment of the entertainment industry, it has become anything but. When we include television, movies, Internet distribution, merchandising, music, overseas sales, live entertainment, and more, we can see in the image below just how much the space has grown in recent years.

In U.S. dollar terms, the industry was worth $22.67 billion in 2023. On a constant currency basis, that represents a massive increase of 126.7% over the previous 10 years. The industry really started to grow around 2014. And a lot of that growth has been from the overseas portion of the space. In fact, since 2018, the overseas portion of the industry has accounted for 61.1% of all the growth that the industry has experienced.

But if the experts are right, the picture is just getting started. According to a different source, in 2023, the overseas anime streaming space was worth about $3.7 billion. Crunchyroll, which used to be owned by telecommunications conglomerate AT&T (T) before it was foolishly sold off to Sony (SONY) in 2021, in a deal valued at $957 million, has exhibited tremendous growth in recent years. It has gone from an estimated 5 million users in August 2021 to over 15 million by August 2024. We also have Netflix (NFLX), which had around 240 titles on its platform as of last year. That still made it significantly smaller than the over 1,800 hosted on Crunchyroll at the same time, but those two firms combined still had an estimated 80% of the overseas anime streaming space.

Historically, a lot of anime has come from other source materials, such as manga. Examples here would include Dandadan, Naruto, Demon Slayer, Frieren: Beyond Journey’s End, The Apothecary Diaries, and more. A recent example, Sakamoto Days is hosted on Netflix and, in the first quarter of 2025 when it came out, racked up 21 million views. But as the space grows, we're seeing greater diversification. Platforms like Netflix and Crunchyroll have come out with some of their own original shows. Netflix, for instance, has had successful original hits such as Great Pretender and Cyberpunk: Edgerunners. Crunchyroll, meanwhile, has had its share of originals as well, and earlier this year announced a joint venture with Aniplex to launch Hayate, Inc, which will serve as an anime production company based in Tokyo that will focus on developing premium original anime content exclusively for the platform.

There's also the opportunity from here to create what I would consider to be anime-adjacent content. Certainly the best successful example of this would be the live-action One Piece series. This was aired in 2023 on Netflix and quickly brought in 62 million views. It was originally inspired by a manga series of the same name that has been in circulation since July 1997.

Of course, that is an outlier. Historically, because of underinvestment and deviation from the source material that fans love, live-action adaptations have a tendency to fall flat and are usually ridiculed by the community. However, despite having an estimated budget of between $17 million and $18 million per episode, the live-action adaptation of One Piece was so successful that Netflix is coming out with season 2 of it next year and is already working on season 3, with plans to go up to 12 seasons at this time. That series is so successful that, even though the original anime series is ongoing, there is a second anime adaptation in the works that will retell the original story from scratch.

According to the estimates provided by different sources, there is the opportunity for significant growth for the anime industry. The current expectation is that, by the year 2033, the overseas portion of the anime and anime-adjacent industry could grow to be worth $42 billion. That's significantly greater than the $11.52 billion the space is currently worth. And that doesn't even factor in the further growth expected of it in its home market of Japan. A different source, which I already cited, figured that by the year 2030, the overseas anime industry itself will be worth $12.5 billion, accounting for the lion's share of the $16 billion that the global anime space will be worth at that time.

By this point, it's clear that Disney understands that it has missed capturing the early mover advantage here. This is why the firm is going more heavily into the space with its relationship with Webtoon Entertainment. However, the entertainment conglomerate is also working on its own anime adaptations. This includes the adaptation of a mobile game called Twisted Wonderland into a three-season series called Twisted Wonderland: The Animation, with the first season set to premiere in October of this year on Disney+. The firm continues to adapt some of its Star Wars content into an anime format, such as with the third volume of Star Wars: Visions, which will air in October of this year as well. In addition to its own content, the company continues to license popular brands to air on not only Disney+, but also on Hulu. Examples here include Bleach: Thousand-Year Blood-War, Attack on Titan, and Fate/stay night: UBW.

Citing another source, I detailed in my other article that focused on Webtoon Entertainment why anime and similar content like that inspired by South Korean manhwa, is distinct from and in many ways more interesting than western animation. The rapid growth that the industry is experiencing can be attributed to its originality and diversity. For instance, according to that source, 29% of U.S. anime viewers said that, "fatigue with Hollywood sequels and remakes" was a major reason why they preferred anime over western content. 39% of them mentioned that they chose anime over western content because of, "unique worlds and stories" that they could be exposed to. And 36% said it was thanks to anime offering a "variety of genres" that you simply don't get in the western world.

A lot of the company’s success will hinge not only on its ability to continue licensing quality content, but perhaps even more importantly on its ability to come out with content of its own that can be popular. This could certainly prove a challenge for a business that has a mixed history when it comes to intellectual property. This does not mean that I'm not optimistic. From my observation, the things that Disney really drops the ball on tend to be content that it does not develop on its own. But the home-grown stuff, such as what it churns out from Pixar and even other non-Pixar content like the Frozen series, has historically been quite successful.

I will say that the firm definitely has a world-class platform that it can rely on to capitalize on the popularity of anime. In the chart above, you can see how many subscribers the business has on Disney+ and how that number has grown over the last few quarters. This includes continued growth domestically, and further international expansion. At the same time, the chart below illustrates that the firm has been able to continue growing average monthly revenue per subscriber on this platform.

This is not all. As I mentioned already, Hulu is also an important distribution hub for the entertainment conglomerate. As the first chart below illustrates, it has also seen growth over the last several quarters. Unfortunately, average revenue per subscriber has come down. But that has to do with the fact that the higher priced SVOD + Live TV part of the business, which is significantly more expensive than just the SVOD portion, has been growing at a slower pace than the SVOD-only part of it has been.

We're continuing to see improvements in revenue and profitability associated with these operations. The entire Direct-to-Consumer part of Disney has seen consistent revenue growth now for the last three quarters. And bottom line results have improved every quarter for at least the last year. In fact, in the third quarter of 2024, the operating loss for the unit was $19 million. But by the third quarter of 2025, it had grown to a profit of $346 million. At this time, when Disney is clearly starting to reap the rewards for its work, is precisely the time when the firm has additional flexibility that it can use to experiment with growth opportunities that are outside its traditional core business.

When it comes to the company more broadly, I will say that I have found myself impressed as of late. In the chart below, you can see financial results for the third quarter of 2025 compared to the same time of 2024. Revenue, profits, and cash flows, have all increased on a year over year basis. The chart also shows results for the first nine months of the 2025 fiscal year compared to the same time of 2024.

The continued growth and turn to profitability for the Direct-to-Consumer Part of the business has absolutely helped it. But as has historically been the case, the big driver, particularly on the bottom line, has been its Experiences segment, which largely centers around its theme parks.

When it comes to the 2025 fiscal year in its entirety, the management team at Disney believes that adjusted earnings per share will come in at around $5.85. That would mean adjusted net profits of $10.52 billion. That would compare favorably to the $10 billion the company reported for last year. And if we assume that the rest of this year looks like the first nine months of the year did on a year over year basis, we would expect adjusted operating cash flow of $18.76 billion and EBITDA of $19.91 billion. Using these estimates, we can see in the chart below how shares of the company are currently priced. These are multiples that, to me, are incredibly attractive. Normally that wouldn't be the case. But when you consider that this is a high-quality business that is the leader in the global entertainment industry, it certainly deserves a premium over most other similar businesses.

Takeaway

Based on the data currently available, I believe there is the opportunity for Disney to generate attractive growth by focusing on the anime industry and adjacent spaces. The firm has been behind where it should be leading up to this point. But what matters most now is that management is finally starting to take this seriously. Rapid growth and the prospect of capturing billions of dollars of additional annual revenue is too much to ignore.

Obviously, there are risks here, and I think investors would be wise to temper their expectations until we see the results of the firm's efforts. The anime and anime-adjacent community is incredibly fickle. And only if Disney can deliver a quality experience that is truly original will it fare well. But the good news is that, even without a significant anime presence, the company is doing quite well right now. Revenue, profits, and cash flows, are all growing nicely, and the firm is trading at multiples that are too low to make sense as I see it. Because of this, I do believe that maintaining it as a "Strong Buy" candidate makes sense.

data.title: Hawthorn Bancshares: Overlooked But Quite Attractive
data.description:
Introduction

Hawthorn Bancshares (NASDAQ:HWBK) is the owner of Hawthorn Bank, a full service bank based in Missouri. With just eighteen offices, this is a relatively small bank with less than $2 billion in assets, but the local focus also seems to indicate the bank is running a more conservative balance sheet as the total amount of loans that are past due remain very low. This allows the bank to keep its loan loss provisions quite low, and that helps the bottom line result.

The low loan loss provisions result in a strong profitability

During the second quarter of this year, the bank saw an interesting combination of a higher interest income and a lower total interest expense. This resulted in a rather substantial increase of the net interest income which jumped by in excess of 10% compared to the second quarter of last year. And while the vast majority of the company’s revenue comes from the net interest income, it also generated $3.5M in non-interest income which helps to offset the approximately $12.3M in non-interest expenses.

This means the pre-tax income reported by the bank (and before taking provisions for loan losses into account) was approximately $7.4M. And as you can see in the income statement above, Hawthorn was able to release a relatively small amount of previously recorded provisions. But the main take away is of course that no additional provisions are needed (and I will discuss the loan book quality and the balance sheet in the next section of this article).

This ultimately resulted in a net profit of approximately $6.1M for an earnings per share of roughly $0.88. This means the total net profit in the first semester of 2025 was approximately $11.5M which represents an EPS of $1.65. And that's just over 25% higher compared to the first half of last year, mainly thanks to the higher net interest income.

The bank currently pays a quarterly dividend of $0.20 per share and this 80 cents on an annualized basis represents a dividend yield of approximately 2.65%. This clearly means the yield is relatively low for the sector, but considering the payout ratio is just under 25% of the net profit (based on the H1 earnings), it is clear the bank is retaining quite a bit of capital which boosts the book value and tangible book value per share.

The balance sheet: robust with low amount of non-performing assets

Looking at the asset side of the balance sheet, the bank has approximately $1.88B in assets, of which $323M is held in liquid assets (cash and AFS debt securities). And given the very low amount of loan loss provisions (and even the recovery of a previously recorded provision in the second quarter and first half of this year), I was very curious to see what the $1.46B loan book consists of.

When you look at the breakdown of the loan book, it becomes apparent that in excess of 50% of the total exposure is related to commercial real estate mortgages. Residential mortgages are a distant second and represent just over 25% of the loan book.

As I explained in previous articles, having a high exposure to commercial real estate is not necessarily a negative element as everything really depends on the risk factor and how stable the borrowers are. Additionally, having a low loan to value ratio would allow a bank to recoup the vast majority, if not the entirety, of its exposure.

The image below shows the breakdown of the loans that are past due and the loans that are no longer accruing. And it becomes immediately clear while the bank no longer has to add any provisions to the existing amount of loan loss provisions. Of the total loan book of $1.46B, less than $2.3M is classified as ‘non-accrual’, with an additional $3.7M in loans that are more than 30 days past due and approximately $0.5M in loans that are more than 90 days past due but are still accruing.

This means the total amount of loans that are past due is approximately $6.4M and this is actually lower than the $6.8M at the end of 2024. Not only does this mean that the current amount of loan loss provisions north of $21 million is more than sufficient to cover the current amount of non-accruing loans, it also explains why the bank was able to recover some of those previously recorded provisions.

At the end of the quarter, Hawthorn Bancshares had a total equity value on the balance sheet of approximately $157M. As there are no intangible assets or goodwill on the balance sheet that number also represents the tangible equity value. Divided over the current share count of approximately 6.9 million shares, this means the TBVPS exceeded $22.50.

Investment thesis

The main question right now is whether or not I would like to pay a premium of just over 30% to the tangible book value per share. The answer is wholeheartedly ‘yes’. As the financial results in a second quarter and the first half of this year have shown, the bank remains very profitable and the fact that it retains the vast majority of its earnings as equity on the balance sheet means the book value per share is increasing at a relatively fast pace. Just to give you an example, the TBVPS at the end of 2024 was approximately $21.38/share while the TBVPS at the end of 2023 and 2022 was $19.33 and $18.04 respectively. And given the recently reported strong earnings results I expect the tangible book value per share to exceed $25 by the end of next year, which would further reduce the current premium to approximately 20%.

Some of the regional and local banks are completely flying under the radar and although I currently have no position yet in Hawthorn Bancshares, I may initiate a long position in the next few months.

data.title: AirJoule's Promising Path From Waste Heat To Water
data.description:
For more than a decade, infrastructure analysts have been monitoring precious water and cooling innovators in hopes that a technology might emerge to both end droughts and facilitate energy savings. AirJoule Technologies (NASDAQ:AIRJ), an early-stage US tech company, says it has found a way. Its AirJoule system is a state of the art atmospheric water harvester fueled by waste heat, developed using laser-focused R&D based on decades of fundamental science and partnered with the heavyweights. The technology combines novel sorbent materials with a vacuum-swing cycle of heat to extract pure water from air in an energy efficient way that is more than 5 times better, by one basic rule-of-thumb estimate, as any existing competing technology.

Recent Financials: Big Paper Profit, Real Cash Burn

More sophisticated investors will do well not to confuse AirJoule's GAAP earnings with its underlying operations. With help from a one-time accounting gain, the company posted a $215.7 million GAAP net profit ($4.15 per share) for 2024. AirJoule contributed its key IP to GE Vernova (GEV) through a 50/50 joint venture in the first quarter of 2024. As an equity-method transaction and a transfer of perpetual licenses, accounting rules required that the fair value of the IP, about $333.5 million, must be recognized as income by AirJoule. The jump is due to one-time transaction costs related to the SPAC merger, which cost $54.7 million in non-recurring fees, as well as ramping up SG&A and R&D for a newly public company. Ex-special items, the underlying cash burn is as follows: $2.02 million for R&D and $9.04 million for G&A in 2024, up from a combined $7.54 million in 2023 when R&D was higher. In other words, cash outflows remain very large indeed.

The company is well-capitalized on the balance sheet, however. It held $30.5 million in cash at the end of the second quarter of 2025, up from $28.0 million at year-end 2024. This was bolstered by a $15 million private placement financing completed on April 25, 2025, which was led by GE Vernova. The capital is intended to accelerate the commercialization of its A250 and A1000 systems. To support its current position, on March 25, 2025, it announced a $30 million committed stock purchase facility with B. Riley over the next 36 months to provide optional capital as needed for their product launch.In total, based on its cash plus this new facility, the company seems to have enough liquidity to last through commercialization at minimum into 2026.

At year end, AirJoule had 30 months of runway on total spend with no financing needed. To support its current position, on March 25, 2025, it announced a $30 million committed stock purchase facility with B. Riley over the next 36 months to provide optional cap as needed for their product launch. In total, based on its cash plus this new facility, the company seems to have enough liquidity to last through commercialization at minimum into 2026. To sum it up, AirJoule 2024 marked a committed step in line with the balance sheet and technical transformation while materializing to zero recurring sales revenue. That $216 million profit was almost entirely a non-cash gain on the JV's IP. The company will now need to convert that gain into shareholder value, which it hopes to do by executing on its operational milestones, which are the deployment and sale of actual hardware. I’m looking for initial pilot deployments in the back half of 2025, early 2026, and a gradual revenue ramp.

Market Backdrop: Parched Earth, Hungry Servers

It might have managed to position itself at the right place at the perfect time as AirJoule seems to enter a market just as stars are aligning in its favor. AWG market segments are headed towards a hyper-growth stage, driven by the expanding footprints of water stress caused by climate change across regions. According to some industry research, the market for global AWG in 2022 amounted to around $3.3 billion, with an expected compound annual growth rate of over 16%, totaling about $12.5 billion by 2031. That surge is prompted by ongoing drought and the increasing demand for decentralized water supplies particularly in arid areas or disaster-prone ones. More than 2 billion people still lack safe water to drink, and at least half of the world's population experiences severe water scarcity for some of the year, according to the United Nations. In regions where freshwater sources are diminished, traditional supply solutions like building reservoirs or drilling wells are becoming either more expensive or politically difficult. Correspondingly, attention is increasingly being drawn to technology-based solutions for providing “water-from-air” as alternative sources of supply.

However, at the same time, some sectors are generating their own water crises. Data centers, the essential infrastructure of the AI and cloud age, are massive water consumers. A 100 MW hyperscale data center in the U.S. can mean as much as two million liters of water a day for cooling. Internationally, data center water usage has surpassed 560B liters a year and is projected to surpass 1.2T by 2030. Since 2022, almost two-thirds of the data centers constructed have been sited in areas under acute water stress (California, Texas and Arizona top the list). In these kinds of locations, data centre operators are exposed to the very real threat that local water restrictions or rapidly increasing costs may limit expansion. From a sustainability perspective, water insecurity erodes reliability and social license to operate. Hence there is a massive incentive for data center operators, and other high heat industries, to find ways to capture waste heat and turn it into valuable water so they can close their cooling loop.

These are the megatrends AirJoule is going after. With its technology it can be installed in facilities that generate a lot of low-grade heat (80–100°C) such as data centers, factories, chemical plants and distilleries etc. to turn their waste thermal energy into distilled water. In a datacenter this translates into capturing the heat that is normally dumped to atmosphere and turning it to cooling water for its evaporative system or even to supply the local water grid. The process generates electricity savings and water production in a single process. This dual benefit is an example of the nexus between the energy transition and climate adaptation. To provide some perspective, essentially 90% of the current AWG market uses refrigeration coils to condense moisture from the air. These condenser-type units are very humidity and temperature dependent, and remain quite energy-intensive, with field data showing energy costs upwards of 2,000 Wh/L or more. AirJoule's test performance of under 160 Wh/L in contrast represents an achievement one order of magnitude ahead. Even allowing for variability in real-world conditions, this performance floor is well below anything else in the market today, and it might just be how we finally de-niche AWG. When it is essentially free waste heat that powers them, the economics radically change.

Strategic Positioning

Fundamentally, the center of the solution is a customized pressure-swing adsorption circuit where MOFs have been utilized as the hygroscopic media. MOFs have very high water adsorption capacity, but due to their cost, they were considered to be impractical. That's where AirJoule has a key breakthrough. Working together with BASF (OTCQX:BASFY), it helped develop a tailored sorbent that was many orders of magnitude cheaper than early MOFs. The company reported the cost of its sorbent dropped from approximately $5,000/kg to around ~$50/kg through scale up of the process. AirJoule’s two-chamber design is in effect “thermally neutral.” Heat that is generated from water sorption in one chamber is used to desorb, or dry the other chamber, meaning only a small net energy input is needed via a low-power vacuum pump and heat loops. By using internal heat recovery, the system boosts efficiency and allows capturing of low temperature waste heat which would otherwise be ineffective when using conventional turbines or flash processes.

Partnerships amplify this technological advantage. AirJoule actually entered into a 50/50 joint venture with GE Vernova in January 2024, uniting its core system with the advanced sorbent coatings by GE. Industry scale products for the commercial markets will be manufactured by this JV. For BASF, the material supplier, producing this new MOF in massive quantities under a global supply agreement ensures materials will not be a bottleneck. At the same time, AirJoule signed a deal to commercialize with Carrier Global's HVAC division. The Carrier agreement is significant because it opens the door for AirJoule's potential integration into Carrier’s extensive air-handling and cooling equipment sales channels throughout the Americas, EMEA, India, and the Middle East. Taking advantage of both their technical collaboration histories and global distribution reach, these relationships and agreements provide AirJoule with the efficiencies it needs to bring its technology to market with little distraction from manufacturing or selling, leaving that component up to its channel partners.

AirJoule has a dual-revenue model that utilizes these partnerships to full advantage. It plans to sell or license big modules on a CapEx basis in industrial outfits like data centers and factories. In the HVAC market, it will probably get royalties or sales as Carrier bundles the technology into its units. There is already a large pipeline of possible projects. In addition to the announced deals, management at AirJoule has also indicated that they are in advanced conversations with several Fortune 500 companies across data centers, energy and industrial sectors to field-test the system. While these exploratory partnerships are non-binding, they provide robust proof points that major global players are seriously evaluating the physics once the units pass muster in the field.

Potential Concerns and Counterpoints

What comes along with the SPAC heritage and company early stage are some legitimate concerns. The company lacks sales revenue. A large GAAP profit in 2024 is misleading, as AirJoule persists with an operational cash consumption. That leaves investors to buy the story and milestones until commercial orders arrive. I get that, I understand that most of the new tech-meet-business bunch falter before finding their wings. But what's different here is some high-profile joint ventures and deep-pocketed backers. GE and Carrier are not small potatoes; they have spent ample R&D dollars and have strong commercial imperatives to extract value from the JV's efforts. In addition, the $30 million equity facility (which is now closed) de-risks to a large extent the potentiality of an imminent cash-flow shortfall.

Also, there is the issue of valuation and dilution. The company has been a highly volatile post-SPAC rocket. Earnout and True-Up shares were recorded as liabilities on the balance sheet and some investors may fear dilution from these issues. In fairness, these share issuances were tied to future performance and they were part of the merger agreement, not unbounded dilution. Specifically, I think there is a disconnect between the company's market capitalization and the value of its underlying assets. AirJoule has a $338.2Mln stake in the JV as reflected in its financial statements, largely representing the value of its contributed IP. If we accept the $333.5 million gain as an approximation of intrinsic enterprise value, that would seem to imply a valuation significantly above today's market cap.

Valuation and Outlook

The dynamic of AirJoule in the pricing can be difficult to articulate at this point, but scenarios can be drawn. The JV sells 100 modules per year by 2027. A price of around $100,000 per module would be about $10m of revenue. A 25-30 percent share of JV profits to AirJoule would yield around $2-3 million in net income. Assuming rapid scaling of 500 units per year by 2030, $50M revenues, and high long-term gross margins, the present value of those cash flows at any conservative discount rate implies a far greater potential valuation than what we have now. This is a rough outline, and upside scenarios associated with the company that are related to faster roll-out or penetration into more HVAC markets could increase these figures.

I would avoid using the naive P/E or EV/Sales multiples as these metrics are useless with respect to AirJoule given its early stage loss-making state. Instead, the stock should be seen as an option on what will likely become a massive revenue stream in five to ten years. The valuation gap should close quickly if AirJoule meets its key milestones in 2025–2026, including the successful pilots, new product announcements from Carrier and early revenue bookings. Conversely, its strategic partnerships, rich IP portfolio, fat cash reserves, and committed financial backing make the risk of total failure pretty small while adoption catches up to speed.

My perspective remains optimistic. AirJoule has nailed all the requisite elements of a game-changing energy-water play – mature core technology that works, blue-chip partners and a critical mass market in need of solutions. There are definite operational hurdles but I believe that these can be overcome based on the team's execution history and means of funding. For now at least, AirJoule is peddling an uncommon commodity; clean drinking water pulled from the atmosphere by waste heat. Even if only implemented mildly, this is a bold promise that would be groundbreaking. My take is experienced execution on early commercial rollouts could put the company in an enviable position to generate substantial shareholder gains over a multi-year horizon especially if industry trends continue.

data.title: Top Value Stocks For August With Steven Cress
data.description:
Get access to the Seeking Alpha PRO Quant Portfolio today!

Join Alpha Picks today!

Daniel Snyder: Hey everyone, Daniel Snyder here from Seeking Alpha. Thank you for tuning in for this hour of diving into six beaten down stocks ready to rally with Steven Cress. Now, I don't know about you, we're going to dive into everything, but one thing that popped in front of my face this morning as well as yours, were are those PPI numbers. So I think Steve might actually touch on that here a little bit today and tell us what's going on with those numbers. Does the market care? Does the market not care? We're going to dive in during this hour, so stick with us. But first things first, let's go ahead and get a quick legal disclaimer out of the way.

We are not advising you personally concerning the nature, potential, value, or suitability of any particular security. You alone are solely responsible for determining whether any investment, security, or strategy, or any product or service is appropriate or suitable for you based on your investment objectives and personal and financial situation. This presentation is for information purposes only. Content is presented as of the day published or indicated and may be superseded by future events. It represents my opinion, Steven Cress's opinions, which may not reflect the views of Seeking Alpha as a whole. Past performance is no guarantee of future results. Seeking Alpha is not a licensed securities dealer, broker, or US investment advisor or investment bank.

And we got that out of the way. Now, Steve, this is one of the -- we were just talking about it before we started this webinar. This is one of those webinars that we love to do. Now, the track record is proven as we put these little mini portfolios together throughout the year. This time it's six stocks, sometimes it's 10 stocks, sometimes it's five stocks. They tend to do pretty well. So I'm ready to dive in, but for the people that may not know who you are, can you give us a quick little background of how you got into the world of finance and where we are today?

Steven Cress: Sure, and Daniel, thank you for organizing this today. So my name is Steve Cress. I'm the Head of Quantitative Strategy at Seeking Alpha. I've been with the firm for about six years, and they actually acquired my firm, which was CressCap Investment Research. I've been in the world of finance for over 30 years. The bulk of my career was at Morgan Stanley, where I ran a prop trading desk in quantitative strategies. And I also founded a hedge fund at one point, which was in quantitative strategies as well. So very strong background there, Daniel, in quantitative strategies. And yeah, I think this morning the market was a little bit unnerved by that PPI number, it and started off later on in the day at a certain point. But I think the market took pause and certainly -- and we'll point out in the presentation, I think a lot of individuals are looking for an interest rate cut and a big cut at that in September.

So I will tell you, if we have a couple more numbers like the PPI data that came out, we may not see that cut. But we'll have to see how it plays out and how the cards fall.

DS: Next week is Jackson Hole, remember? So everybody's watching out for the commentary from the Fed and, obviously, with a PPI as hot as a summer in Florida, if you will. I mean, this is not a good setup going into it, but we'll see what happens.

SC: Yeah. And normally, you know, that Jackson Hole meeting, like everybody's waiting for it, it's a bit of a snooze. Like nothing really comes out of it. But, look, over the next few weeks, we're going to see more data points come out and it is getting to that point where if you did expect inflation to start kicking in because of the tariffs, this is the point where it would start kicking in. The question is, are there enough offsets to it? The tax reform, will that stimulate the economy enough? Will that create inflation if the economy is stimulated? So there's a lot to see. A lot of economists do feel with the tariffs that it will be a one-time type of event. So it won't have a long tail to it. Others believe though that it will have a long tail. So we'll just have to see how the data falls.

So a little bit about our presentation today. I'll go over Quant a little bit. I'll review the pull and push factors that are driving the market right now. And then we'll dive right into the six beaten down stocks. And as Daniel said, we have a pretty good track record at highlighting beaten down stocks that typically are Quant Strong Buys, for various reasons these stocks fall. And usually it's a bit short-term oriented, that decline. And it's a great way to take advantage of stocks on the dip.

So we'll dive right into it. Daniel, what is Quant? How do we achieve our results? Well, Quant is a data-driven methodology that is used for investment research. It's really not dissimilar to what a regular fundamental analyst would do at Morgan Stanley or Goldman Sachs. We do look at the core factors, which are value, growth, and profitability. The only difference is, and it's a big difference, when we look for those factors, we use the power of computer processing to identify all stocks with those factors and we compare those companies to other similar companies. So it gives us the ability through that computer processing to assess thousands and thousands of stocks, where a single analyst typically really can only cover maybe 15 or 20 companies. And when they write research reports, they'll typically issue maybe three or four reports per stock a year. With Quant, we actually refresh those recommendations every single day.

So we literally download millions and millions of data points. We refresh it and every day we have a new directional recommendation. So it could be a Strong Buy for a long time. It could be a Hold for a long time. But if those data points change, being that we refresh it every day, we'll capture that change. And if there is a shift, it will be apparent in that directional recommendation.

So not all Quant is the same. Some Quant models look just for momentum. Some look just for value. Some look just for growth. We have a diversified approach. We refer to what a GARP methodology, which is usually referred to as growth at a reasonable price. And really the core metrics that we try to identify are growth, value, profitability, positive analyst EPS revisions, and momentum. Those are the core factors that we look for in our model. And it has proven to be successful over time. And in fact, we'll be able to share that to you.

And the point of this chart is, we don't have an ETF at Seeking Alpha, or mutual fund, but what we do do is, we monitor the performance of all our Strong Buys every single day. And any stock that's a Strong Buy, we throw it into a portfolio. If it falls out of the Strong Buy category, it comes out of the portfolio. So here we monitor our Strong Buys against the S&P 500. And really going back since 2010, we've outperformed the S&P 500 every single year, but more recently and that's what's always important, people want to look at recent, our track record is very good in the last 52 weeks, the Strong Buys are up 40% versus the S&P which is up 13.63% for the same period. And Wall Street Strong Buys are actually up 19.9%. So I will say it's actually fairly rare for Wall Street analysts to outperform the S&P 500. Over the last decade, the S&P 500 has handily beaten Wall Street Analyst. But I think being that the concentration risk that's been in the S&P 500 with the Mag 7 stocks, that hasn't really played out that year -- this year as it has in past years. So it's given Wall Street analysts the ability to recommend stocks outside of the Mag 7 and they've actually performed fairly well compared to the S&P 500, but of course not nearly as well as Seeking Alpha's Strong Buys.

DS: Steve, real quick, I want to jump in. There's a great question just came in from Jeffrey asking, what is the difference between the quant ratings to the Seeking Alpha recommendation versus like say Seeking Alpha analysts, I guess. And the big thing that we want to communicate here is, the Seeking Alpha analysts rating is the entire community of people writing on Seeking Alpha about the stock. And as an aggregate rating of the last 30 to 90 days for that individual stock, where your system here is strictly data driven. There is no human bias. There is no analyst jumping in and saying, well, I think a catalyst is coming. You are looking strictly at the fundamental data and comparing it against peers in the same sector every single morning. So just wanted to take a quick second to clarify that.

SC: Yeah, and what I did is, went to our platform, so people know what Jeffrey's referring to. On the ratings summary, you will see that there is the Seeking Alpha Analyst consensus rating, there's the Wall Street consensus rating, and the Quant rating. These are three independent investment research resources, so you should look at it as three independent sources. And in this case, all three have a Strong Buy on this particular stock. But as Daniel mentioned with the Quant, we refresh it every single day. So if any of these factors change, it impacts the total Quant score. And that Quant score could change if there were a change in these factors. I’ll take us back to our presentation, Daniel.

DS: And that Quant rating system runs every single morning before the market. So you always have fresh grades the day...

SC: Absolutely. Spot on, spot on. All right. So pull and push factors driving the market. So we have a nice chart to the right side here, which is the July jobs report, which came in lower than expected. Some of the other data outside of that, we've had rising core inflation, core CPI accelerated to 3.1%. It's actually its highest level since February 2025. So that was definitely concerning. We saw wholesale prices are -- the PPI index come in at 0.09, which just came out earlier today that Daniel was referring to. That was higher than expected. And of course, there are escalating trade tensions again. President Trump's new reciprocal tariff triggered a 22% jump in the VIX and pulled major indices lower on August 2nd. The market did recover, but it's just beginning to show you that, you know, it is -- people are getting a little bit nervous. So the market did bring us back to a new high this morning. Definitely scared off a little bit, but the market adjusted to it.

What's it all about? People are focused really on inflation and interest rates. And when it comes to interest rates, we look at what interest rate traders are doing. So I think this is really interesting. If you look on the right side, on the top, you'll see a bar chart. And that was just as of July 16. And at that point, 56% of traders expected a rate cut in September. But since that point in time, if you look at it as of August 14th, we now have 90% of interest rate traders expecting a rate cut for September 17th. So, Daniel, my belief is, and I think a number of market strategists really feel, one of the reasons why the market's going to new highs has to do with the belief that interest rates could be coming down.

So, were we to see interest rates come down, that would satisfy the market. I would say if we don't see interest rates come down in September, and most interest rate traders are expecting that rate cut, and a lot of people who are investors have been expecting that rate cut, that could be pretty disappointing for the market, so we don't get that.

DS: The surprising thing for me is, go back through history and look at every time that the Fed has cut interest rates when the market was at an all-time high and it won't make you feel very good. So it feels like these upcoming data points are going to be watched like a hawk in the weeks ahead and especially coming off of the wrap up -- we're wrapping up earnings, right? The last big one is Nvidia coming here at the end of the month, but earnings season has been great. So to the Fed, they're saying, why do I need to cut? So all eyes are on this in the weeks ahead and it's definitely going to probably add a little volatility.

SC: I think you're absolutely right. I think it’ll add volatility. It's a very anticipated rate cut. Could happen that you get the rate cut and then like the market already anticipated, so nothing really happens. Of course, the downside risk being that there is no cut and then the market would be like, whoa, what's going on? So we'll see what happens. But I'll tell you what, as we get into the stocks, we really try not to pay attention to too many macro factors. We look at the data behind each stock and that's how we make our decisions.

So as we were talking about a couple of the potential tripwires that could occur, there could be tariff fallout, there could be, as I said, the Fed's -- a potential Fed surprise of delaying the cuts or no cuts at all. And as we've seen the market appreciate here, we are seeing equity evaluations stretch out. And they are quite elevated versus our five and 10 year averages at this point.

So bottom line, really, I want to tell people, try not to pay too much attention to these factors. If the market does correct at some point, look at these stocks. The economy is still in fairly good shape. Corporations are doing a good job coming in better than expected with earnings. So that dip could really work towards your favor. Don't look at it negatively. But at this point in time, we already have some stocks that have taken a dip. In fact, we have six beaten down stocks that have Strong Buy recommendations. And that's what I want to highlight here today.

So Our first one is FinVolution Group, ticker symbol FINV. Now this won't be for the faint of heart because this is actually a Chinese company that we're highlighting. But in terms of looking at the core data, when you look at valuation and growth for the company, it ranks number three out of 38 within consumer finance, and the financials overall rate - ranks eight out of 691. As I mentioned, it's a China-based fintech company that connects borrowers with banks and other financial institutions. So we're looking for stocks that have dipped. This one is down 16% month to date, despite really surging expansion, a very, very powerful AI marketing tool for this company.

I'm going to take us actually into the platform. So we're going to go to FinVolution. As I highlighted before, our three independent sources here, being Seeking Alpha contributors, and Wall Street and Quant, all rate this stock a Strong Buy. So it sort of hit the trifecta of Strong Buys. I'd mentioned on the month, it's down 18% at this point, adding another 2.43%, so continuing with that downward trend.

So why do we like this stock? First off, on valuation, it gets an A+. This stock is dirt cheap versus the rest of the financials. If you look at the forward PE on it, as I mentioned, it's an A+. The forward PE on the stock is 5.49x. The sector is at 11.9x. So the stock is at a 50% discount to the sector. So on a forward PE basis, it's very, very cheap versus the sector. If we look at some of the other factors such as EV to EBITDA, EV to EBIT, it's got straight As across the board. If we look at price to cash flow, it's an A-, 5.18x versus the sector at 9.7x, very cheap. So it's definitely attractive from a valuation standpoint.

If we look at it from a growth standpoint, it has a B-, but we will see if we look at EPS diluted growth forward. So this is using consensus estimates from analysts, taking the forward growth rate, they have a growth rate of 17.5% for the company versus other financials at a growth rate of 9.9%. So the growth is at a 76% premium to the sector. And again, this is forward EPS growth. This is not looking at the historical. The year-over-year number was 26% versus the sector at 12%. But when I look at growth, I like to look forward. So we're looking at forward EPS growth using the consensus of Wall Street analysts, and they have 17.5% growth rate. So that is a very strong growth rate for the company.

In terms of profitability, if you look at our return on equity, which I always look to -- look at for financial institutions, it's at 17.8% versus the sector at 10.68%. So it is a 67% premium to the sector for return on equity, return on capital with an A+, return on total assets with an A+, and even the EBITDA margin is an A at 54%. So the company really looking strong across most of the metrics, almost a straight A, the growth is a little bit lower with a B-, but still incredibly strong. So that's FinVolution, ticker symbol, FINV.

Back to our presentation, Daniel. And our next stock is Sezzle. So we came out with this recommendation actually recently for one of our portfolio products, PQP. And boy, the stock got hammered almost right away, which doesn't happen often, but it does happen. So reviewing the stock here, Sezzle has a market cap of about $3 billion, and this is in financials as well. And in the sector, it's 26 out of 691. So not as high as the previous selection, but within its industry of transaction and payment processing, it ranks number one out of 41.

This company's business is payment solutions provider, and it has really gotten hit hard. I'm going to take us to the platform. So we're going to look at Sezzle here and you can see over the last month, the stock is down 32%, so getting hit quite hard. And Daniel I will tell you they came out with their numbers on August 7th and they beat expectations. They beat both top line and bottom line. So there was a nice beat there. So it was a bit of a surprise that the stock came off so sharply. There was a short squeeze on the stock recently. A lot of people feel like it's a little bit of a reversion to the mean with the short squeeze. If you take a look at the stock year-to-date, you can see it's up 114%. So the fact that it's fallen over the last month by about 32% it's pretty small in comparison to the increase that it's had on the overall year. So that short squeeze is starting to unwind, but a lot of people believe because of that squeeze the stock really ran up. So a bit of a reversion to the mean.

Again, they beat their estimates. Seeking Alpha contributors do have a Hold on it, but Wall Street coming with a Strong Buy, and a pretty Strong Buy, very, very Strong Buy at that with consensus, if we go down. You can see there are three professional analysts that cover the company, and they all have a Strong Buy on the company.

If we were to look at the growth, we can see it's an A+. So Sezzle year-over-year, the growth was 146%. And then if we look at the long-term growth, and this particular factor here, which is EPS forward long-term using a three to five year CAGR, so that's analysts going pretty far out. They're looking for this company to grow at 245% versus the sector at a mere 10%. So that is a huge, huge growth number for this company. So there are high expectations from professional analysts.

If we're to look at the valuation for the company, we could see it is a bit expensive. Overall, it comes in at a B- and the reason why it's a B- in the face of the PEs which come into the D category is the PEG ratio, Daniel. So you can see the PEG ratio is an A-. This is a very strong predictive factor, PEG. You can see that -- and the PEG for those who are not familiar with it, it's a great factor because it actually combines the growth rate with the PE valuation. So it really puts the valuation in perspective of a company's growth by tying the two together and blending it. And you can see on a PEG basis, on a forward PEG basis, it's 0.11 versus the sector at 1.27. So in terms of this valuation metric, it's at a 90% discount to the sector. So it's really quite telling when you combine that growth number in with a PE. So that's absolute PE basis. Yes, it does look expensive. But if you take that PE and you combine it with growth, it looks quite attractive.

In terms of profitability, we're looking at an EBIT margin of 56% versus the sector at 22%. And we're looking at an outstanding return on equity, absolutely huge at 115% versus the sector at 10%. And again, as I mentioned when I look at financials, I really do like to look at return on equity and that is a very, very strong number.

So Sezzle, ticker symbol, SEZL. Again, this stock was really doing quite well up until the last month. And the last month, the stock has come down 32%. But again, that's in light of the stock being up 114% year-to-date, could have something to do with that short squeeze.

DS: This is -- yeah, this one's interesting to me, right? Because we do these webinars all the time and we present stocks and a lot of people are like, well, why do I buy at the 52 week high when a stock’s at all time high? So this one for me, I think I'm going to personally put on my watch list and keep an eye on it because either this is a great buying opportunity with that pullback, especially with like you were mentioning, beating earnings. And if you go into their earnings call, like I was just kind of using the earnings AI feature we have here on Seeking Alpha, looking over the transcript and stuff. It looks to be a lot of positives. So might be one of the market shakeup moments here with this stock. That's an interesting one, Steve.

SC: Yeah. And I'm actually glad that you mentioned, like, looking at the stock now versus a while back. If you look at the valuation grade, you can actually see the valuation grade now is a B- versus six months ago it was a D-. So, despite the stock rallying 114% year-to-date, the stock actually has a better valuation framework now than it did six months ago. So I'm really happy that you cued people into that because it's a better valuation framework now than it was back then.

All right, back to our slideshow. All right, our next stock is a REIT, which is Host Hotels & Resorts, ticker symbol HST. This is a big company, a market cap of $11.21 billion, within the real estate sector, it ranks four out of 175. And within its sector of hotels and resorts, it actually ranks one out of 15. This is a global premier REIT. It's down 3.9% month-to-date, despite a double beat, which was reported on July 30th. So beating both top line and bottom line. Some of the attractive features here, AFFO growth is 54% versus the sector at 4.6%. And the AFFO payout ratio comes in at about 55% versus a negative 25% for the sector, that's crazy. They have a tremendous amount of cash at $1.43 billion in cash from operations, huge premium to the sector. And the dividend yield at 5.59% has a dividend safety grade of an A. So I'll point that out. If you look to the right, you can see the dividend safety grade is an A. Typically any company that we recommend that has a dividend, if that dividend safety grade falls between a B- and an A+ it's historically shown to be a very, very safe dividend. The territory that you want to watch out for -- is any dividend safety grade that's F through C-. So being a dividend -- with a dividend safety grade of A, I think most people can expect to rely on that dividend.

Our number four stock is Oddity Tech. So this is actually not a tech company, although, it kind of is. It's within the consumer staples sector. Market cap is $3.3 billion. It is a Quant Strong Buy. Within consumer staples, it ranks one out of 178. And within its sector, Personal Care Products it ranks one out of 10. This is a direct-to-consumer beauty company operating exclusively online, hence the technology component to it. Recent post earnings sold off due to slightly disappointing Q3 margin. So I'm actually going to take us our platform on this. So we're going to go into Oddity.

DS: Yeah. Even JP Morgan put out their own little release about their 20% sell off here. But that's what we're talking about, the six beaten down stocks. This one's beaten down.

SC: This one is beaten down and hopefully it's going to be a good opportunity. The consensus from our Seeking Alpha contributors is a Buy. Wall Street consensus is a Buy. The Quant has a Strong Buy in the stock. The valuation, I will say is a little bit expensive on this one. So when we look at the factor grades, I want to highlight to you these factor grades are all sector relative. So when you're looking at this company within consumer staples, and you look at these core investment characteristics here, you can see, these grades indicate, it gives you sort of an instant characterization actually of how it compares to the sector. So versus the sector, it's a little bit expensive, but the growth versus the sector is an A. Profitability versus the sector B+. Momentum and revisions are A versus the sector. But really within one click, you could take a deeper dive.

So when I say valuation is expensive and we clicked on it, you will see by looking at the PE and EV to sales, what the grades are. But again, what brings it up a little bit, Daniel, is that PEG ratio, that forward PEG ratio coming in at an A. So if you were to look at the PEG ratio, the valuation framework actually looks inexpensive using that one metric.

If we were to go to growth, it looks pretty darn good. If you look at the EPS diluted growth going forward, it is a 21% growth rate. Consumer staples has a growth rate of 5.89%. So this is at a huge, huge premium in terms of growth to the sector, and that's absolutely what you want. So whether you're looking at EPS on a diluted basis or EPS on a GAAP basis, it's way stronger than the sector. And then if you look at one of the metrics I'd like to look at, the EPS forward long-term three to five-year CAGR, that's a tremendous premium to the sector as well. So you're not going to find many consumer staple companies with this kind of growth rate. So as we saw from that PEG ratio, it's kind of worth the valuation. Again, this ranks number two out of 31 within personal care products and number 13 out of 178 for consumer staples overall. But I really do like the way this company looks. If we go to the summary page.

DS: Steve, I was just looking at the – their earnings here because they just announced as well, they raised both their net revenue and guidance, and their profit guidance going into the fiscal year. So obviously, they know -- we don't know.

SC: As you mentioned too, Oddity’s 20% sell-off defies the numbers, JP Morgan. So the numbers look great. And short interest was a bit high in the stock, coming at 13.2%. So part of that run-up that we could have seen, I'm not completely sure, but that could have been a short squeeze. So maybe after the numbers, a little bit of a reversion to the mean usually ends up being a great buying opportunity for people who are not sure of this stock.

So taking us back to the slideshow, going to stock number five, another consumer staple company. This is a European company Henkel, ticker symbol HENOY. This is a really big company. Market cap is $32 billion. Currently within consumer staples sector it ranks 11 out of 178. And within its industry of household products, it ranks 2 out of 13. It is a German conglomerate specifically serving both households and industrial markets. The share price slid due to disappointing Q2 earnings release. So it is a consumer staples company. So sometimes you can miss by a penny or two and it can take a stock way down, especially within consumer staples. But this company for the most part is, as I said, it's tremendous. And I will actually take us to the platform so we could see what it looks like with the numbers specifically.

So valuation is attractive at a B versus the sector. Let's take a look at the growth. So I think what's important with a growth is if we look at the EPS Forward growth rate, Wall Street consensus, again, Daniel, this isn't my estimate for growth. This is actually Wall Street consensus. They're looking at earnings growing at a rate of 24% versus the sector at 8.28%. So that is a huge, huge premium to the sector. Part of what we're seeing is that the year-over-year growth rate was 53%. So it's slowing down to a forward growth rate of 24%. I think maybe that got realized recently. So the stock took a little bit of a dip. But even at 24% growth versus the sector, that is absolutely tremendous.

If we were to look at the profitability for this company, it's fantastic. The gross profit margins are 50% versus the sector at 36%. And the EBIT margin is 14% versus the sector at 9.5%. So looking really nice against the sector. And another - a number that really sticks out to me is actually the cash per share. So you can see it has an A grade versus the sector.
